title: History of Earth

infobox.value: 4,540–4,000 | Earth is formed out of debris around the solar [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk"). There is no life. Temperatures are extremely hot, with frequent volcanic activity and hellish-looking environments (hence the eon's name, which comes from [Hades](/wiki/Hades "Hades")). The atmosphere is nebular. Possible early oceans or bodies of liquid water. The Moon is formed around this time probably due to a [protoplanet's collision into Earth](/wiki/Giant-impact_hypothesis "Giant-impact hypothesis").

4,000–2,500 | [Prokaryote](/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote") life, the first form of life, emerges at the very beginning of this eon, in a process known as [abiogenesis](/wiki/Abiogenesis "Abiogenesis"). The continents of [Ur](/wiki/Ur_(continent) "Ur (continent)"), [Vaalbara](/wiki/Vaalbara "Vaalbara") and [Kenorland](/wiki/Kenorland "Kenorland") may have existed around this time. The atmosphere is composed of volcanic and greenhouse gases.

2,500–538.8 | The name of this eon means "early life". [Eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), a more complex form of life, emerge, including some forms of [multicellular organisms](/wiki/Multicellular_organism "Multicellular organism"). [Bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") begin producing oxygen, shaping the third and current of Earth's atmospheres. Plants, later animals and possibly earlier forms of fungi form around this time. The early and late phases of this eon may have undergone "[Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth")" periods, in which all of the planet suffered below-zero temperatures. The early continents of [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_(supercontinent) "Columbia (supercontinent)"), [Rodinia](/wiki/Rodinia "Rodinia") and [Pannotia](/wiki/Pannotia "Pannotia"), in that order, may have existed in this eon.

538.8–present | [Complex life](/wiki/Complex_life "Complex life"), including [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate"), begin to dominate Earth's ocean in a process known as the [Cambrian explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_explosion "Cambrian explosion"). [Pangaea](/wiki/Pangaea "Pangaea") forms and later dissolves into [Laurasia](/wiki/Laurasia "Laurasia") and [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana"), which in turn dissolve into the current continents. Gradually, life expands to land and familiar forms of plants, animals and fungi begin appearing, including annelids, insects and reptiles, hence the eon's name, which means "visible life". Several [mass extinctions](/wiki/Mass_extinction "Mass extinction") occur, among which birds, the descendants of non-avian dinosaurs, and more recently mammals emerge. Modern animals—[including humans](/wiki/Human_evolution "Human evolution")—evolve at the most recent phases of this eon.

concerns the development of [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") from its formation to the present day.[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1)[[2]](#cite_note-TimeScale-2) Nearly all branches of [natural science](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science") have contributed to understanding of the main events of Earth's past, characterized by constant [geological](/wiki/Geology "Geology") change and biological [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

For more detail of the geological history of Earth, see [Geological history of Earth](/wiki/Geological_history_of_Earth "Geological history of Earth").

For more detail of the biological history of Earth, see [History of life](/wiki/History_of_life "History of life").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Geologic_Clock_with_events_and_periods.svg/330px-Geologic_Clock_with_events_and_periods.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Geologic_Clock_with_events_and_periods.svg)

Earth's history with time-spans of the [eons](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale "Geologic time scale") to scale. [Ma](/wiki/Year#Abbreviations_yr_and_ya "Year") means "million years ago", [Ga](/wiki/Year#Abbreviations_yr_and_ya "Year") means "billion years ago".

The natural **history of Earth** concerns the development of [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") from its formation to the present day.[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1)[[2]](#cite_note-TimeScale-2) Nearly all branches of [natural science](/wiki/Natural_science "Natural science") have contributed to understanding of the main events of Earth's past, characterized by constant [geological](/wiki/Geology "Geology") change and biological [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution").

The [geological time scale](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale "Geologic time scale") (GTS), as defined by international convention,[[3]](#cite_note-3) depicts the large spans of time from the beginning of Earth to the present, and its divisions chronicle some definitive events of Earth history. Earth formed around 4.54 billion years ago, approximately one-third the [age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe"), by [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)") from the [solar nebula](/wiki/Formation_and_evolution_of_the_Solar_System#Formation_of_the_planets "Formation and evolution of the Solar System").[[4]](#cite_note-USGS1997-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Volcanic [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing") probably created the primordial [atmosphere](/wiki/Atmosphere "Atmosphere") and then the ocean, but the early atmosphere contained almost no [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"). Much of Earth was molten because of frequent collisions with other bodies which led to extreme volcanism. While Earth was in its earliest stage ([Early Earth](/wiki/Early_Earth "Early Earth")), a giant impact collision with a planet-sized body named [Theia](/wiki/Theia_(planet) "Theia (planet)") is thought to have formed the Moon. Over time, Earth cooled, causing the formation of a solid [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)"), and allowing liquid water on the surface.

The [Hadean](/wiki/Hadean "Hadean") eon represents the time before a reliable (fossil) record of life; it began with the formation of the planet and ended 4.0 billion years ago. The following [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean") and [Proterozoic](/wiki/Proterozoic "Proterozoic") eons produced the [beginnings of life](/wiki/Abiogenesis "Abiogenesis") on Earth and its earliest [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution"). The succeeding eon is the [Phanerozoic](/wiki/Phanerozoic "Phanerozoic"), divided into three eras: the [Palaeozoic](/wiki/Palaeozoic "Palaeozoic"), an era of arthropods, fishes, and the first life on land; the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic"), which spanned the rise, reign, and climactic extinction of the non-avian dinosaurs; and the [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic"), which saw the rise of mammals. Recognizable humans emerged at most 2 million years ago, a vanishingly small period on the geological scale.

The [earliest undisputed evidence of life on Earth](/wiki/Earliest_known_life_forms "Earliest known life forms") dates at least from 3.5 billion years ago,[[7]](#cite_note-Origin1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Origin2-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) during the [Eoarchean](/wiki/Eoarchean "Eoarchean") Era, after a geological crust started to solidify following the earlier molten Hadean eon. There are [microbial mat](/wiki/Microbial_mat "Microbial mat") [fossils](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") such as [stromatolites](/wiki/Stromatolites "Stromatolites") found in 3.48 billion-year-old [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") discovered in [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia").[[10]](#cite_note-AP-20131113-10)[[11]](#cite_note-TG-20131113-JP-11)[[12]](#cite_note-AST-20131108-12) Other early physical evidence of a [biogenic substance](/wiki/Biogenic_substance "Biogenic substance") is [graphite](/wiki/Graphite "Graphite") in 3.7 billion-year-old [metasedimentary rocks](/wiki/Metasediment "Metasediment") discovered in southwestern [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland")[[13]](#cite_note-NG-20131208-13) as well as "remains of [biotic life](/wiki/Biotic_life "Biotic life")" found in 4.1 billion-year-old rocks in Western Australia.[[14]](#cite_note-AP-20151019-14)[[15]](#cite_note-PNAS-20151014-pdf-15) According to one of the researchers, "If life arose relatively quickly on Earth … then it could be common in the [universe](/wiki/Universe "Universe")."[[14]](#cite_note-AP-20151019-14)

[Photosynthetic](/wiki/Photosynthetic "Photosynthetic") organisms appeared between 3.2 and 2.4 billion years ago and began enriching the atmosphere with oxygen. [Life](/wiki/Life "Life") remained mostly small and microscopic until about [580 million years ago](/wiki/Timeline_of_human_evolution#Unicellular_life "Timeline of human evolution"), when complex [multicellular life](/wiki/Multicellular_life "Multicellular life") arose, developed over time, and culminated in the [Cambrian Explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_Explosion "Cambrian Explosion") about 538.8 million years ago. This sudden diversification of life forms produced most of the major phyla known today, and divided the Proterozoic Eon from the Cambrian Period of the Paleozoic Era. It is estimated that 99 percent of all species that ever lived on Earth, over five billion,[[16]](#cite_note-Book-Biology-16) have gone [extinct](/wiki/Extinction "Extinction").[[17]](#cite_note-StearnsStearns2000-17)[[18]](#cite_note-NYT-20141108-MJN-18) Estimates on the number of Earth's current [species](/wiki/Species "Species") range from 10 million to 14 million,[[19]](#cite_note-MillerSpoolman2012-19) of which about 1.2 million are documented, but over 86 percent have not been described.[[20]](#cite_note-PLoS-20110823-20)

Earth's crust has constantly changed since its formation, as has life since its first appearance. Species continue to [evolve](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution"), taking on new forms, splitting into daughter species, or going extinct in the face of ever-changing physical environments. The process of [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics") continues to shape Earth's continents and oceans and the life they harbor.

## Eons

In [geochronology](/wiki/Geochronology "Geochronology"), time is generally measured in [mya](/wiki/Million_years_ago "Million years ago") (million years ago), each unit representing the period of approximately 1,000,000 years in the past. The history of Earth is divided into four great [eons](/wiki/Geologic_time_scale#Divisions_of_geologic_time "Geologic time scale"), starting 4,540 mya with the formation of the planet. Each eon saw the most significant changes in Earth's composition, climate and life. Each eon is subsequently divided into eras, which in turn are divided into periods, which are further divided into epochs.

| Eon | Time (mya) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Hadean](/wiki/Hadean "Hadean") | 4,540–4,000 | Earth is formed out of debris around the solar [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk"). There is no life. Temperatures are extremely hot, with frequent volcanic activity and hellish-looking environments (hence the eon's name, which comes from [Hades](/wiki/Hades "Hades")). The atmosphere is nebular. Possible early oceans or bodies of liquid water. The Moon is formed around this time probably due to a [protoplanet's collision into Earth](/wiki/Giant-impact_hypothesis "Giant-impact hypothesis"). |
| [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean") | 4,000–2,500 | [Prokaryote](/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote") life, the first form of life, emerges at the very beginning of this eon, in a process known as [abiogenesis](/wiki/Abiogenesis "Abiogenesis"). The continents of [Ur](/wiki/Ur_(continent) "Ur (continent)"), [Vaalbara](/wiki/Vaalbara "Vaalbara") and [Kenorland](/wiki/Kenorland "Kenorland") may have existed around this time. The atmosphere is composed of volcanic and greenhouse gases. |
| [Proterozoic](/wiki/Proterozoic "Proterozoic") | 2,500–538.8 | The name of this eon means "early life". [Eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), a more complex form of life, emerge, including some forms of [multicellular organisms](/wiki/Multicellular_organism "Multicellular organism"). [Bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") begin producing oxygen, shaping the third and current of Earth's atmospheres. Plants, later animals and possibly earlier forms of fungi form around this time. The early and late phases of this eon may have undergone "[Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth")" periods, in which all of the planet suffered below-zero temperatures. The early continents of [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_(supercontinent) "Columbia (supercontinent)"), [Rodinia](/wiki/Rodinia "Rodinia") and [Pannotia](/wiki/Pannotia "Pannotia"), in that order, may have existed in this eon. |
| [Phanerozoic](/wiki/Phanerozoic "Phanerozoic") | 538.8–present | [Complex life](/wiki/Complex_life "Complex life"), including [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate"), begin to dominate Earth's ocean in a process known as the [Cambrian explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_explosion "Cambrian explosion"). [Pangaea](/wiki/Pangaea "Pangaea") forms and later dissolves into [Laurasia](/wiki/Laurasia "Laurasia") and [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana"), which in turn dissolve into the current continents. Gradually, life expands to land and familiar forms of plants, animals and fungi begin appearing, including annelids, insects and reptiles, hence the eon's name, which means "visible life". Several [mass extinctions](/wiki/Mass_extinction "Mass extinction") occur, among which birds, the descendants of non-avian dinosaurs, and more recently mammals emerge. Modern animals—[including humans](/wiki/Human_evolution "Human evolution")—evolve at the most recent phases of this eon. |

## Solar System formation

Main article: [Formation and evolution of the Solar System](/wiki/Formation_and_evolution_of_the_Solar_System "Formation and evolution of the Solar System")

See also: [Planetary differentiation](/wiki/Planetary_differentiation "Planetary differentiation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Protoplanetary-disk.jpg/500px-Protoplanetary-disk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protoplanetary-disk.jpg)

An artist's rendering of a [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk")

The standard model for the formation of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") (including [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")) is the [solar nebula hypothesis](/wiki/Nebular_hypothesis "Nebular hypothesis").[[22]](#cite_note-22) In this model, the Solar System formed from a large, rotating cloud of interstellar dust and gas called the [solar nebula](/wiki/Solar_nebula "Solar nebula"). It was composed of [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium") created [shortly after](/wiki/Recombination_(cosmology) "Recombination (cosmology)") the [Big Bang](/wiki/Big_Bang "Big Bang") 13.8 [Ga](/wiki/Gigaannum#SI_prefix_multipliers "Gigaannum") (billion years ago) and heavier [elements](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") ejected by [supernovae](/wiki/Supernovae "Supernovae"). About 4.5 [Ga](/wiki/A_(year) "A (year)"), the nebula began a contraction that may have been triggered by the [shock wave](/wiki/Shock_wave "Shock wave") from a nearby [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova").[[23]](#cite_note-Matson-23) A shock wave would have also made the nebula rotate. As the cloud began to accelerate, its [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum"), [gravity](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation"), and [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia") flattened it into a [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk") perpendicular to its axis of rotation. Small [perturbations](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)") due to collisions and the angular momentum of other large debris created the means by which kilometer-sized [protoplanets](/wiki/Protoplanet "Protoplanet") began to form, orbiting the nebular center.[[24]](#cite_note-Goldreich1973-24)

The center of the nebula, not having much angular momentum, collapsed rapidly, the compression heating it until [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion") of hydrogen into helium began. After more contraction, a [T Tauri star](/wiki/T_Tauri_star "T Tauri star") ignited and evolved into the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"). Meanwhile, in the outer part of the nebula gravity caused [matter](/wiki/Matter "Matter") to condense around density perturbations and dust particles, and the rest of the protoplanetary disk began separating into rings. In a process known as runaway [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)"), successively larger fragments of dust and debris clumped together to form planets.[[24]](#cite_note-Goldreich1973-24) Earth formed in this manner about 4.54 billion years ago (with an [uncertainty](/wiki/Measurement_uncertainty "Measurement uncertainty") of 1%)[[25]](#cite_note-age_earth1c-25)[[26]](#cite_note-age_of_earth_faq-26)[[4]](#cite_note-USGS1997-4) and was largely completed within 10–20 million years.[[27]](#cite_note-Yin-27) In June 2023, scientists reported evidence that the planet Earth may have formed in just three million years, much faster than the 10−100 million years thought earlier.[[28]](#cite_note-WP-202130616-28)[[29]](#cite_note-NAT-20230614-29) Nonetheless, the [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind") of the newly formed T Tauri star cleared out most of the material in the disk that had not already condensed into larger bodies. The same process is expected to produce [accretion disks](/wiki/Accretion_disks "Accretion disks") around virtually all newly forming stars in the universe, some of which yield [planets](/wiki/Extrasolar_planet "Extrasolar planet").[[30]](#cite_note-Kokubo2002-30)

The proto-Earth grew by accretion until its interior was hot enough to melt the heavy, [siderophile](/wiki/Siderophile_element "Siderophile element") [metals](/wiki/Metal "Metal"). Having higher [densities](/wiki/Density "Density") than the silicates, these metals sank. This so-called *[iron catastrophe](/wiki/Iron_catastrophe "Iron catastrophe")* resulted in the separation of a [primitive mantle](/wiki/Primitive_mantle "Primitive mantle") and a (metallic) core only 10 million years after Earth began to form, producing the [layered structure of Earth](/wiki/Internal_structure_of_Earth "Internal structure of Earth") and setting up the formation of [Earth's magnetic field](/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field "Earth's magnetic field").[[31]](#cite_note-31) J.A. Jacobs was the first to suggest that [Earth's inner core](/wiki/Earth%27s_inner_core "Earth's inner core")—a solid center distinct from the liquid [outer core](/wiki/Earth%27s_outer_core "Earth's outer core")—is [freezing](/wiki/Earth%27s_inner_core#Growth "Earth's inner core") and growing out of the liquid outer core due to the gradual cooling of Earth's interior (about 100 degrees Celsius per billion years[[32]](#cite_note-32)).[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Hadean and Archean Eons

Main articles: [Hadean](/wiki/Hadean "Hadean") and [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Earth_formation.jpg/250px-Earth_formation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Earth_formation.jpg)

Artist's conception of [Hadean Eon](/wiki/Hadean_Eon "Hadean Eon") Earth, when it was much hotter and inhospitable to all forms of life.

The first [eon](/wiki/Eon_(geology) "Eon (geology)") in Earth's history, the *Hadean*, begins with Earth's formation and is followed by the *Archean* eon at 3.8 Ga.[[2]](#cite_note-TimeScale-2): 145 The oldest rocks found on Earth date to about 4.0 Ga, and the oldest [detrital](/wiki/Detrital "Detrital") [zircon](/wiki/Zircon "Zircon") crystals in rocks to about 4.4 Ga,[[34]](#cite_note-nature1-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Lindsey-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Cavosie-36) soon after the formation of Earth's [crust](/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") and Earth itself. The [giant impact hypothesis](/wiki/Giant_impact_hypothesis "Giant impact hypothesis") for the Moon's formation states that shortly after formation of an initial crust, the proto-Earth was impacted by a smaller protoplanet, which ejected part of the [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(geology) "Mantle (geology)") and crust into space and created the Moon.[[37]](#cite_note-belbruno-37)[[38]](#cite_note-Carsten-38)[[39]](#cite_note-moonwalk-39)

From [crater counts](/wiki/Crater_count "Crater count") on other celestial bodies, it is inferred that a period of intense meteorite impacts, called the *[Late Heavy Bombardment](/wiki/Late_Heavy_Bombardment "Late Heavy Bombardment")*, began about 4.1 Ga, and concluded around 3.8 Ga, at the end of the Hadean.[[40]](#cite_note-space.com-bombardment-40) In addition, volcanism was severe due to the large [heat flow](/wiki/Heat_flow "Heat flow") and [geothermal gradient](/wiki/Geothermal_gradient "Geothermal gradient").[[41]](#cite_note-Green-41) Nevertheless, detrital zircon crystals dated to 4.4 Ga show evidence of having undergone contact with liquid water, suggesting that Earth already had oceans or seas at that time.[[34]](#cite_note-nature1-34)

By the beginning of the Archean, Earth had cooled significantly. Present life forms could not have survived at Earth's surface, because the Archean atmosphere lacked [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") hence had no [ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer") to block ultraviolet light. Nevertheless, it is believed that primordial life began to evolve by the early Archean, with candidate [fossils](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") dated to around 3.5 Ga.[[42]](#cite_note-Taylor-2006-42) Some scientists even speculate that life could have begun during the early Hadean, as far back as 4.4 Ga, surviving the possible Late Heavy Bombardment period in [hydrothermal vents](/wiki/Hydrothermal_vents "Hydrothermal vents") below Earth's surface.[[43]](#cite_note-reuters1-43)

### Formation of the Moon

Main articles: [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), [Origin of the Moon](/wiki/Origin_of_the_Moon "Origin of the Moon"), and [Giant-impact hypothesis](/wiki/Giant-impact_hypothesis "Giant-impact hypothesis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Artist%27s_concept_of_collision_at_HD_172555.jpg/250px-Artist%27s_concept_of_collision_at_HD_172555.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artist%27s_concept_of_collision_at_HD_172555.jpg)

Artist's impression of the enormous collision that probably formed the Moon

Earth's only [natural satellite](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), the Moon, is larger relative to its planet than any other satellite in the Solar System.[[nb 1]](#cite_note-46) During the [Apollo program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program"), rocks from the Moon's surface were brought to Earth. [Radiometric dating](/wiki/Radiometric_dating "Radiometric dating") of these rocks shows that the Moon is 4.53 ± 0.01 billion years old,[[46]](#cite_note-Kleine-47) formed at least 30 million years after the Solar System.[[47]](#cite_note-Halliday-48) New evidence suggests the Moon formed even later, 4.48 ± 0.02 Ga, or 70–110 million years after the start of the Solar System.[[48]](#cite_note-halliday-2008-49)

Theories for the formation of the Moon must explain its late formation as well as the following facts. First, the Moon has a low density (3.3 times that of water, compared to 5.5 for Earth[[49]](#cite_note-earth_fact_sheet-50)) and a small metallic core. Second, Earth and Moon have the same oxygen [isotopic signature](/wiki/Isotopic_signature "Isotopic signature") (relative abundance of the oxygen isotopes). Of the theories proposed to account for these phenomena, one is widely accepted: The *giant impact hypothesis* proposes that the Moon originated after a body the size of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") (sometimes named [Theia](/wiki/Theia_(planet) "Theia (planet)")[[47]](#cite_note-Halliday-48)) struck the proto-Earth a glancing blow.[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1): 256[[50]](#cite_note-StarChild-51)[[51]](#cite_note-Canup-52)

The collision released about 100 million times more energy than the more recent [Chicxulub impact](/wiki/Chicxulub_crater "Chicxulub crater") that is believed to have caused the extinction of the non-avian dinosaurs. It was enough to vaporize some of Earth's outer layers and melt both bodies.[[50]](#cite_note-StarChild-51)[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1): 256 A portion of the mantle material was [ejected](/wiki/Ejecta "Ejecta") into orbit around Earth. The giant impact hypothesis predicts that the Moon was depleted of metallic material,[[52]](#cite_note-Liu-53) explaining its abnormal composition.[[53]](#cite_note-Newsom-54) The ejecta in orbit around Earth could have condensed into a single body within a couple of weeks. Under the influence of its own gravity, the ejected material became a more spherical body: the Moon.[[54]](#cite_note-Taylor-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Hadean.png/250px-Hadean.png)](/wiki/File:Hadean.png)

Artist's impression of a Hadean landscape with the relatively newly formed Moon still looming closely over Earth and both bodies sustaining strong [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism").

### First continents

[![Map with color and texture](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/North_america_terrain_2003_map.jpg/250px-North_america_terrain_2003_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_america_terrain_2003_map.jpg)

Geologic map of North America, color-coded by age. From most recent to oldest, age is indicated by yellow, green, blue, and red. The reds and pinks indicate rock from the [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean").

Plate tectonics is driven by [mantle convection](/wiki/Mantle_convection "Mantle convection"), the flow of heated rock from Earth's interior to the surface.[[55]](#cite_note-DaviesMantle-56): 2 The rising mantle extruded at [mid-oceanic ridges](/wiki/Mid-oceanic_ridge "Mid-oceanic ridge") builds up rigid [tectonic plates](/wiki/Tectonic_plate "Tectonic plate"), which are eventually shifted to [subduction zones](/wiki/Subduction_zone "Subduction zone") where they sink back into the mantle. During the early Archean (about 3.0 Ga) the mantle was probably around 1,600 °C (2,910 °F) hotter than today,[[56]](#cite_note-Cattermole-57): 82 so convection in the mantle was faster, leading to a faster tectonic process during the Hadean and Archean. Subduction zones were more common and tectonic plates smaller.[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1): 258[[57]](#cite_note-58)

The initial crust, which formed when Earth's surface first solidified, totally disappeared from a combination of this fast Hadean plate tectonics and the intense impacts of the Late Heavy Bombardment. It is thought the original crust was [basaltic](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt") like today's [oceanic crust](/wiki/Oceanic_crust "Oceanic crust"), because little crustal differentiation had yet taken place.[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1): 258 The first larger pieces of [continental crust](/wiki/Continental_crust "Continental crust"), formed of lighter elements which float upward during [partial melting](/wiki/Partial_melting "Partial melting") in the lower crust, appeared at the end of the Hadean, about 4.0 Ga. What is left of these first small continents are called [cratons](/wiki/Craton "Craton"); they form the cores around which today's continents grew.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

The [oldest rocks](/wiki/Oldest_dated_rocks "Oldest dated rocks") on Earth are found in the [North American craton](/wiki/North_American_craton "North American craton") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). They are [tonalites](/wiki/Tonalite "Tonalite") from about 4.0 Ga. They show traces of [metamorphism](/wiki/Metamorphism "Metamorphism") by high temperature, but also sedimentary grains that have been rounded by erosion during transport by water, showing that rivers and seas existed then.[[59]](#cite_note-Lunine-60) Cratons consist primarily of two alternating types of [terranes](/wiki/Terrane "Terrane"). The first are so-called [greenstone belts](/wiki/Greenstone_belt "Greenstone belt"), consisting of low-grade metamorphosed sedimentary rocks. These "greenstones" are similar to the sediments today found in [oceanic trenches](/wiki/Oceanic_trench "Oceanic trench"), above subduction zones, suggesting the start of subduction during the Archean. The second type is a complex of [felsic](/wiki/Felsic "Felsic") [magmatic rocks](/wiki/Magmatic_rock "Magmatic rock"), mostly tonalite, [trondhjemite](/wiki/Trondhjemite "Trondhjemite") or [granodiorite](/wiki/Granodiorite "Granodiorite"), similar to [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite"): such terranes are called TTG-terranes. TTG-complexes are seen as the [relicts](/wiki/Relict_(geology) "Relict (geology)") of the first continental crust, formed by partial melting in basalt.[[60]](#cite_note-Condie-61): Chapter 5

### Oceans and atmosphere

See also: [Origin of water on Earth](/wiki/Origin_of_water_on_Earth "Origin of water on Earth") and [Prebiotic atmosphere](/wiki/Prebiotic_atmosphere "Prebiotic atmosphere")

Earth is often described as having had three atmospheres. The first, captured from the solar nebula, was composed of light ([atmophile](/wiki/Atmophile "Atmophile")) elements from the solar nebula, mostly hydrogen and helium. A combination of the solar wind and Earth's heat would have driven off this atmosphere, and today's atmosphere is depleted of these elements compared to cosmic abundances.[[61]](#cite_note-Kasting93-62) After the impact which created the Moon, the molten Earth released volatile gases; and later more gases were released by [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano"), completing a second atmosphere rich in [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") but poor in oxygen.[[1]](#cite_note-Stanley2005-1): 256 Finally, the third atmosphere, rich in oxygen, emerged when bacteria [began to produce oxygen](/wiki/Great_Oxygenation_Event "Great Oxygenation Event") about 2.8 Ga.[[62]](#cite_note-Gale-63): 83–84, 116–117

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/NASA-EarlyEarth-PaleOrangeDot-20190802.jpg/250px-NASA-EarlyEarth-PaleOrangeDot-20190802.jpg)](/wiki/File:NASA-EarlyEarth-PaleOrangeDot-20190802.jpg)

*The pale orange dot*, an artist's impression of the [early Earth](/wiki/Early_Earth "Early Earth") which might have appeared orange through its [hazy](/wiki/Haze "Haze") [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") rich [*prebiotic second atmosphere*](/wiki/Prebiotic_atmosphere "Prebiotic atmosphere").[[63]](#cite_note-Trail_Elsila_Müller_Lyons_p.-64)[[64]](#cite_note-Astrobiology_2017-65) Earth's atmosphere at this stage was somewhat comparable to today's [atmosphere of Titan](/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Titan "Atmosphere of Titan").[[65]](#cite_note-Trainer_Pavlov_DeWitt_Jimenez_pp._18035–18042-66)

In early models for the formation of the atmosphere and ocean, the second atmosphere was formed by outgassing of [volatiles](/wiki/Volatile_(astrogeology) "Volatile (astrogeology)") from Earth's interior. Now it is considered likely that many of the volatiles were delivered during accretion by a process known as *impact degassing* in which incoming bodies vaporize on impact. The ocean and atmosphere would, therefore, have started to form even as Earth formed.[[66]](#cite_note-Kasting03-67) The new atmosphere probably contained [water vapor](/wiki/Water_vapor "Water vapor"), carbon dioxide, nitrogen, and smaller amounts of other gases.[[67]](#cite_note-Kasting-2006-68)

Planetesimals at a distance of 1 [astronomical unit](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (AU), the distance of Earth from the Sun, probably did not contribute any water to Earth because the solar nebula was too hot for ice to form and the hydration of rocks by water vapor would have taken too long.[[66]](#cite_note-Kasting03-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Selsis-69) The water must have been supplied by meteorites from the outer asteroid belt and some large planetary embryos from beyond 2.5 AU.[[66]](#cite_note-Kasting03-67)[[69]](#cite_note-Morbidelli-70) Comets may also have contributed. Though most comets are today in orbits farther away from the Sun than [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"), computer simulations show that they were originally far more common in the inner parts of the Solar System.[[59]](#cite_note-Lunine-60): 130–132

As Earth cooled, [clouds](/wiki/Cloud "Cloud") formed. Rain created the oceans. Recent evidence suggests the oceans may have begun forming as early as 4.4 Ga.[[34]](#cite_note-nature1-34) By the start of the Archean eon, they already covered much of Earth. This early formation has been difficult to explain because of a problem known as the [faint young Sun paradox](/wiki/Faint_young_Sun_paradox "Faint young Sun paradox"). Stars are known to get brighter as they age, and the Sun has become 30% brighter since its formation 4.5 billion years ago.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Many models indicate that the early Earth should have been covered in ice.[[71]](#cite_note-Sagan-72)[[66]](#cite_note-Kasting03-67) A likely solution is that there was enough carbon dioxide and methane to produce a [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect"). The carbon dioxide would have been produced by volcanoes and the methane by early microbes. It is hypothesized that there also existed an organic haze created from the products of methane photolysis that caused an [anti-greenhouse effect](/wiki/Anti-greenhouse_effect "Anti-greenhouse effect") as well.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Another greenhouse gas, [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"), would have been ejected by volcanos but quickly destroyed by ultraviolet radiation.[[62]](#cite_note-Gale-63): 83
