Main article: [Abiogenesis](/wiki/Abiogenesis "Abiogenesis")

One of the reasons for interest in the early atmosphere and ocean is that they form the conditions under which life first arose. There are many models, but little consensus, on how life emerged from non-living chemicals; chemical systems created in the laboratory fall well short of the minimum complexity for a living organism.[[73]](#cite_note-Szathmary-74)[[74]](#cite_note-Luisi-75)

The first step in the emergence of life may have been chemical reactions that produced many of the simpler [organic](/wiki/Organic_chemistry "Organic chemistry") compounds, including [nucleobases](/wiki/Nucleobases "Nucleobases") and [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acids "Amino acids"), that are the building blocks of life. An [experiment in 1952](/wiki/Miller%E2%80%93Urey_experiment "Miller–Urey experiment") by [Stanley Miller](/wiki/Stanley_Miller "Stanley Miller") and [Harold Urey](/wiki/Harold_Urey "Harold Urey") showed that such molecules could form in an atmosphere of water, methane, ammonia and hydrogen with the aid of sparks to mimic the effect of [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning").[[75]](#cite_note-Lazcano-76) Although atmospheric composition was probably different from that used by Miller and Urey, later experiments with more realistic compositions also managed to synthesize organic molecules.[[76]](#cite_note-NYTimes-77) [Computer simulations](/wiki/Computer_simulation "Computer simulation") show that [extraterrestrial organic molecules](/wiki/Abiogenesis#Observed_extraterrestrial_organic_molecules "Abiogenesis") could have formed in the protoplanetary disk before the formation of Earth.[[77]](#cite_note-Space-20120329-78)

Additional complexity could have been reached from at least three possible starting points: [self-replication](/wiki/Self-replication "Self-replication"), an organism's ability to produce offspring that are similar to itself; [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism"), its ability to feed and repair itself; and external [cell membranes](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane"), which allow food to enter and waste products to leave, but exclude unwanted substances.[[78]](#cite_note-Pereto-79)

#### Replication first: RNA world

Main article: [RNA world](/wiki/RNA_world "RNA world")

Even the simplest members of the [three modern domains](/wiki/Three-domain_system "Three-domain system") of life use [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") to record their "recipes" and a complex array of [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") and [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") molecules to "read" these instructions and use them for growth, maintenance, and self-replication.

The discovery that a kind of RNA molecule called a [ribozyme](/wiki/Ribozyme "Ribozyme") can [catalyze](/wiki/Catalyst "Catalyst") both its own replication and the construction of proteins led to the hypothesis that earlier life-forms were based entirely on RNA.[[79]](#cite_note-Joyce-80) They could have formed an [RNA world](/wiki/RNA_world "RNA world") in which there were individuals but no [species](/wiki/Species "Species"), as [mutations](/wiki/Mutation "Mutation") and [horizontal gene transfers](/wiki/Horizontal_gene_transfer "Horizontal gene transfer") would have meant that the offspring in each generation were quite likely to have different [genomes](/wiki/Genomes "Genomes") from those that their parents started with.[[80]](#cite_note-Hoenigsberg-81) RNA would later have been replaced by DNA, which is more stable and therefore can build longer genomes, expanding the range of capabilities a single organism can have.[[81]](#cite_note-Forterre-82) Ribozymes remain as the main components of [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome"), the "protein factories" of modern cells.[[82]](#cite_note-Cech-83)

Although short, self-replicating RNA molecules have been artificially produced in laboratories,[[83]](#cite_note-Johnston-84) doubts have been raised about whether natural non-biological synthesis of RNA is possible.[[84]](#cite_note-Levy-85)[[85]](#cite_note-Larralde-86)[[86]](#cite_note-Lindahl-87) The earliest ribozymes may have been formed of simpler [nucleic acids](/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid") such as [PNA](/wiki/Peptide_nucleic_acid "Peptide nucleic acid"), [TNA](/wiki/Threose_nucleic_acid "Threose nucleic acid") or [GNA](/wiki/Glycerol_nucleic_acid "Glycerol nucleic acid"), which would have been replaced later by RNA.[[87]](#cite_note-Orgel-88)[[88]](#cite_note-Nelson-89) Other [pre-RNA replicators](/wiki/Peptide-RNA_world "Peptide-RNA world") have been posited, including [crystals](/wiki/Crystal "Crystal")[[89]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Watchmaker-90): 150 and even quantum systems.[[90]](#cite_note-Daviesb-91)

In 2003 it was proposed that porous metal sulfide [precipitates](/wiki/Precipitate "Precipitate") would assist RNA synthesis at about 100 °C (212 °F) and at ocean-bottom pressures near [hydrothermal vents](/wiki/Hydrothermal_vent "Hydrothermal vent"). In this hypothesis, the proto-cells would be confined in the pores of the metal substrate until the later development of lipid membranes.[[91]](#cite_note-Martin-92)

#### Metabolism first: iron–sulfur world

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/DNA_replication_split.svg/250px-DNA_replication_split.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DNA_replication_split.svg)

The replicator in virtually all known life is [deoxyribonucleic acid](/wiki/Deoxyribonucleic_acid "Deoxyribonucleic acid"). DNA is far more complex than the original replicator and its replication systems are highly elaborate.

Main article: [Iron–sulfur world hypothesis](/wiki/Iron%E2%80%93sulfur_world_hypothesis "Iron–sulfur world hypothesis")

Another long-standing hypothesis is that the first life was composed of protein molecules. Amino acids, the building blocks of [proteins](/wiki/Proteins "Proteins"), are easily synthesized in plausible prebiotic conditions, as are small [peptides](/wiki/Peptides "Peptides") ([polymers](/wiki/Polymers "Polymers") of amino acids) that make good catalysts.[[92]](#cite_note-Kauffman-93): 295–297 A series of experiments starting in 1997 showed that amino acids and peptides could form in the presence of [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") and [hydrogen sulfide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide") with [iron sulfide](/wiki/Iron(II)_sulfide "Iron(II) sulfide") and [nickel sulfide](/wiki/Nickel_sulfide "Nickel sulfide") as catalysts. Most of the steps in their assembly required temperatures of about 100 °C (212 °F) and moderate pressures, although one stage required 250 °C (482 °F) and a pressure equivalent to that found under 7 kilometers (4.3 mi) of rock. Hence, self-sustaining synthesis of proteins could have occurred near hydrothermal vents.[[93]](#cite_note-Wachtershauser-94)

A difficulty with the metabolism-first scenario is finding a way for organisms to evolve. Without the ability to replicate as individuals, aggregates of molecules would have "compositional genomes" (counts of molecular species in the aggregate) as the target of natural selection. However, a recent model shows that such a system is unable to evolve in response to natural selection.[[94]](#cite_note-Vasas-95)

#### Membranes first: Lipid world

It has been suggested that double-walled "bubbles" of [lipids](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid") like those that form the external membranes of cells may have been an essential first step.[[95]](#cite_note-Trevors01-96) Experiments that simulated the conditions of the early Earth have reported the formation of lipids, and these can spontaneously form [liposomes](/wiki/Liposome "Liposome"), double-walled "bubbles", and then reproduce themselves. Although they are not intrinsically information-carriers as nucleic acids are, they would be subject to [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") for longevity and reproduction. Nucleic acids such as RNA might then have formed more easily within the liposomes than they would have outside.[[96]](#cite_note-Segre-97)

#### The clay theory

Further information: [Graham Cairns-Smith § Clay hypothesis](/wiki/Graham_Cairns-Smith#Clay_hypothesis "Graham Cairns-Smith")

Some [clays](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), notably [montmorillonite](/wiki/Montmorillonite "Montmorillonite"), have properties that make them plausible accelerators for the emergence of an RNA world: they grow by self-replication of their crystalline pattern, are subject to an analog of [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection") (as the clay "species" that grows fastest in a particular environment rapidly becomes dominant), and can catalyze the formation of RNA molecules.[[97]](#cite_note-Cairns-Smith-98) Although this idea has not become the scientific consensus, it still has active supporters.[[98]](#cite_note-Ferris-99): 150–158[[89]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Watchmaker-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/96/Liposome_cross_section.png)](/wiki/File:Liposome_cross_section.png)

Cross-section through a [liposome](/wiki/Liposome "Liposome")

Research in 2003 reported that montmorillonite could also accelerate the conversion of [fatty acids](/wiki/Fatty_acid "Fatty acid") into "bubbles", and that the bubbles could encapsulate RNA attached to the clay. Bubbles can then grow by absorbing additional lipids and dividing. The formation of the earliest [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") may have been aided by similar processes.[[99]](#cite_note-Hanczyc-100)

A similar hypothesis presents self-replicating iron-rich clays as the progenitors of [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide"), lipids and amino acids.[[100]](#cite_note-Hartman-101)

#### Last universal common ancestor

Main article: [Last universal common ancestor](/wiki/Last_universal_common_ancestor "Last universal common ancestor")

It is believed that of this multiplicity of protocells, only one [line](/wiki/Lineage_(evolution) "Lineage (evolution)") survived. Current [phylogenetic](/wiki/Phylogenetics "Phylogenetics") evidence suggests that the [last universal ancestor](/wiki/Last_universal_ancestor "Last universal ancestor") (LUA) lived during the early [Archean](/wiki/Archean "Archean") eon, perhaps 3.5 Ga or earlier.[[101]](#cite_note-:0-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103) This LUA cell is the ancestor of all life on Earth today. It was probably a [prokaryote](/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote"), possessing a cell membrane and probably ribosomes, but lacking a [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus") or membrane-bound [organelles](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle") such as [mitochondria](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion") or [chloroplasts](/wiki/Chloroplast "Chloroplast"). Like modern cells, it used DNA as its genetic code, RNA for information transfer and [protein synthesis](/wiki/Protein_synthesis "Protein synthesis"), and enzymes to [catalyze reactions](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis"). Some scientists believe that instead of a single organism being the last universal common ancestor, there were populations of organisms exchanging genes by [lateral gene transfer](/wiki/Lateral_gene_transfer "Lateral gene transfer").[[103]](#cite_note-Penny-LUCA-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Archean.png/330px-Archean.png)](/wiki/File:Archean.png)

Artist's impression of Earth during the later Archean, the largely cooled [planetary crust](/wiki/Earth%27s_crust "Earth's crust") and water-rich barren [surface](/wiki/Planetary_surface "Planetary surface"), marked by [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") and [continents](/wiki/Continent "Continent"), features already [round](/wiki/Stromatolite "Stromatolite") [microbialites](/wiki/Microbialite "Microbialite"). The Moon, still orbiting Earth much closer than today and still dominating Earth's sky, produced strong [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide").[[104]](#cite_note-Lunar_and_Planetary_Institute-105)

## Proterozoic Eon

Main article: [Proterozoic](/wiki/Proterozoic "Proterozoic")

The Proterozoic eon lasted from 2.5 Ga to 538.8 Ma (million years) ago.[[105]](#cite_note-StratChart_2022-106) In this time span, [cratons](/wiki/Cratons "Cratons") grew into continents with modern sizes. The change to an oxygen-rich atmosphere was a crucial development. Life developed from prokaryotes into [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") and multicellular forms. The Proterozoic saw a couple of severe ice ages called [Snowball Earths](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth"). After the last Snowball Earth about 600 Ma, the evolution of life on Earth accelerated. About 580 Ma, the [Ediacaran biota](/wiki/Ediacaran_biota "Ediacaran biota") formed the prelude for the [Cambrian Explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_Explosion "Cambrian Explosion").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Oxygen revolution

Main article: [Great Oxidation Event](/wiki/Great_Oxidation_Event "Great Oxidation Event")

See also: [Ozone layer](/wiki/Ozone_layer "Ozone layer")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Lake_Thetis-Stromatolites-LaRuth.jpg/250px-Lake_Thetis-Stromatolites-LaRuth.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Thetis-Stromatolites-LaRuth.jpg)

[Lithified](/wiki/Lithification "Lithification") [stromatolites](/wiki/Stromatolite "Stromatolite") on the shores of [Lake Thetis](/wiki/Lake_Thetis "Lake Thetis"), [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"). Archean stromatolites are the first direct fossil traces of life on Earth.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Banded_Iron_Formation_Barberton.jpg/250px-Banded_Iron_Formation_Barberton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banded_Iron_Formation_Barberton.jpg)

A [banded iron formation](/wiki/Banded_iron_formation "Banded iron formation") from the 3.15 Ga [Moodies Group](/wiki/Moodies_Group "Moodies Group"), [Barberton Greenstone Belt](/wiki/Barberton_Greenstone_Belt "Barberton Greenstone Belt"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"). Red layers represent the times when oxygen was available; gray layers were formed in anoxic circumstances.

The earliest cells absorbed energy and food from the surrounding environment. They used [fermentation](/wiki/Fermentation_(biochemistry) "Fermentation (biochemistry)"), the breakdown of more complex compounds into less complex compounds with less energy, and used the energy so liberated to grow and reproduce. Fermentation can only occur in an *anaerobic* (oxygen-free) environment. The evolution of [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis") made it possible for cells to derive energy from the Sun.[[106]](#cite_note-CondieSystem-107): 377

Most of the life that covers the surface of Earth depends directly or indirectly on photosynthesis. The most common form, oxygenic photosynthesis, turns carbon dioxide, water, and sunlight into food. It captures the energy of sunlight in energy-rich molecules such as ATP, which then provide the energy to make sugars. To supply the electrons in the circuit, hydrogen is stripped from water, leaving oxygen as a waste product.[[107]](#cite_note-Leslie-108) Some organisms, including [purple bacteria](/wiki/Purple_bacteria "Purple bacteria") and [green sulfur bacteria](/wiki/Green_sulfur_bacteria "Green sulfur bacteria"), use an [anoxygenic form of photosynthesis](/wiki/Anoxygenic_photosynthesis "Anoxygenic photosynthesis") that uses alternatives to hydrogen stripped from water as [electron donors](/wiki/Electron_donors "Electron donors"); examples are hydrogen sulfide, sulfur and iron. Such [extremophile](/wiki/Extremophile "Extremophile") organisms are restricted to otherwise inhospitable environments such as hot springs and hydrothermal vents.[[106]](#cite_note-CondieSystem-107): 379–382[[108]](#cite_note-Nisbet-109)

The simpler anoxygenic form arose about 3.8 Ga, not long after the appearance of life. The timing of oxygenic photosynthesis is more controversial; it had certainly appeared by about 2.4 Ga, but some researchers put it back as far as 3.2 Ga.[[107]](#cite_note-Leslie-108) The latter "probably increased global productivity by at least two or three orders of magnitude".[[109]](#cite_note-De-Marais-photosynthesis-110)[[110]](#cite_note-Olson-2006-111) Among the oldest remnants of oxygen-producing lifeforms are fossil [stromatolites](/wiki/Stromatolite "Stromatolite").[[109]](#cite_note-De-Marais-photosynthesis-110)[[110]](#cite_note-Olson-2006-111)[[111]](#cite_note-Holland-2006-112)

At first, the released oxygen was bound up with [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), and other minerals. The oxidized iron appears as red layers in geological strata called [banded iron formations](/wiki/Banded_iron_formations "Banded iron formations") that formed in abundance during the [Siderian](/wiki/Siderian "Siderian") period (between 2500 Ma and 2300 Ma).[[2]](#cite_note-TimeScale-2): 133 When most of the exposed readily reacting minerals were oxidized, oxygen finally began to accumulate in the atmosphere. Though each cell only produced a minute amount of oxygen, the combined metabolism of many cells over a vast time transformed Earth's atmosphere to its current state. This was Earth's third atmosphere.[[112]](#cite_note-ForteyDtL-113): 50–51[[62]](#cite_note-Gale-63): 83–84, 116–117

Some oxygen was stimulated by solar ultraviolet radiation to form [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone"), which collected in a layer near the upper part of the atmosphere. The ozone layer absorbed, and still absorbs, a significant amount of the ultraviolet radiation that once had passed through the atmosphere. It allowed cells to colonize the surface of the ocean and eventually the land: without the ozone layer, ultraviolet radiation bombarding land and sea would have caused unsustainable levels of mutation in exposed cells.[[113]](#cite_note-cosmic-evolution-bio1-114)[[59]](#cite_note-Lunine-60): 219–220

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Oxygenation-atm.svg/330px-Oxygenation-atm.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Oxygenation-atm.svg)

Graph showing range of estimated [partial pressure](/wiki/Partial_pressure "Partial pressure") of atmospheric oxygen through geologic time[[111]](#cite_note-Holland-2006-112)

Photosynthesis had another major impact. Oxygen was toxic; much life on Earth probably died out as its levels rose in what is known as the *[oxygen catastrophe](/wiki/Oxygen_catastrophe "Oxygen catastrophe")*. Resistant forms survived and thrived, and some developed the ability to use oxygen to increase their metabolism and obtain more energy from the same food.[[113]](#cite_note-cosmic-evolution-bio1-114)

### Snowball Earth

Main article: [Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Snowball_Huronian.jpg/250px-Snowball_Huronian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Snowball_Huronian.jpg)

Artist's rendition of an oxinated fully-frozen [Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth") with no remaining liquid surface water.

The [natural evolution of the Sun](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution") made it progressively more [luminous](/wiki/Luminosity "Luminosity") during the Archean and Proterozoic eons; the Sun's luminosity increases 6% every billion years.[[59]](#cite_note-Lunine-60): 165 As a result, Earth began to receive more heat from the Sun in the Proterozoic eon. However, Earth did not get warmer. Instead, the geological record suggests it cooled dramatically during the early Proterozoic. [Glacial deposits](/wiki/Glacial_deposit "Glacial deposit") found in South Africa date back to 2.2 Ga, at which time, based on [paleomagnetic](/wiki/Paleomagnetism "Paleomagnetism") evidence, they must have been located near the equator. Thus, this glaciation, known as the [Huronian glaciation](/wiki/Huronian_glaciation "Huronian glaciation"), may have been global. Some scientists suggest this was so severe that Earth was frozen over from the poles to the equator, a hypothesis called Snowball Earth.[[114]](#cite_note-Snowball-115)

The Huronian ice age might have been caused by the [increased oxygen concentration](/wiki/Great_Oxygenation_Event "Great Oxygenation Event") in the atmosphere, which caused the decrease of methane (CH4) in the atmosphere. Methane is a strong greenhouse gas, but with oxygen it reacts to form CO2, a less effective greenhouse gas.[[59]](#cite_note-Lunine-60): 172 When free oxygen became available in the atmosphere, the concentration of methane could have decreased dramatically, enough to counter the effect of the increasing heat flow from the Sun.[[115]](#cite_note-SnowballCause-116)

However, the term Snowball Earth is more commonly used to describe later extreme ice ages during the [Cryogenian](/wiki/Cryogenian "Cryogenian") period. There were four periods, each lasting about 10 million years, between 750 and 580 million years ago, when Earth is thought to have been covered with ice apart from the highest mountains, and average temperatures were about −50 °C (−58 °F).[[116]](#cite_note-117) The snowball may have been partly due to the location of the supercontinent [Rodinia](/wiki/Rodinia "Rodinia") straddling the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). Carbon dioxide combines with rain to weather rocks to form carbonic acid, which is then washed out to sea, thus extracting the greenhouse gas from the atmosphere. When the continents are near the poles, the advance of ice covers the rocks, slowing the reduction in carbon dioxide, but in the Cryogenian the weathering of Rodinia was able to continue unchecked until the ice advanced to the tropics. The process may have finally been reversed by the emission of carbon dioxide from volcanoes or the destabilization of methane [gas hydrates](/wiki/Gas_hydrates "Gas hydrates"). According to the alternative [Slushball Earth](/wiki/Slushball_Earth "Slushball Earth") theory, even at the height of the ice ages there was still open water at the Equator.[[117]](#cite_note-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119)

### Emergence of eukaryotes

Further information: [Eukaryote § Origin of eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote#Origin_of_eukaryotes "Eukaryote")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Plagiomnium_affine_laminazellen.jpeg/250px-Plagiomnium_affine_laminazellen.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Plagiomnium_affine_laminazellen.jpeg)

Chloroplasts in the cells of a moss

Modern [taxonomy](/wiki/Taxonomy_(biology) "Taxonomy (biology)") classifies life into three domains. The time of their origin is uncertain. The [Bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") domain probably first split off from the other forms of life (sometimes called [Neomura](/wiki/Neomura "Neomura")), but this supposition is controversial. Soon after this, by 2 Ga,[[119]](#cite_note-SciAm-eukaryote-120) the Neomura split into the [Archaea](/wiki/Archaea "Archaea") and the [Eukaryota](/wiki/Eukaryota "Eukaryota"). Eukaryotic cells (Eukaryota) are larger and more complex than prokaryotic cells (Bacteria and Archaea), and the origin of that complexity is only now becoming known.[[120]](#cite_note-121) The earliest fossils possessing features typical of [fungi](/wiki/Fungus "Fungus") date to the [Paleoproterozoic](/wiki/Paleoproterozoic "Paleoproterozoic") era, some 2.4 Ga ago; these multicellular [benthic](/wiki/Benthic "Benthic") organisms had filamentous structures capable of [anastomosis](/wiki/Anastomosis "Anastomosis").[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Around this time, the first [proto-mitochondrion](/wiki/Proto-mitochondrion "Proto-mitochondrion") was formed. A bacterial cell related to today's *[Rickettsia](/wiki/Rickettsia "Rickettsia")*,[[122]](#cite_note-Andersson-123) which had evolved to [metabolize oxygen](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"), entered a larger prokaryotic cell, which lacked that capability. Perhaps the large cell attempted to digest the smaller one but failed (possibly due to the evolution of prey defenses). The smaller cell may have tried to [parasitize](/wiki/Parasite "Parasite") the larger one. In any case, the smaller cell survived inside the larger cell. Using oxygen, it metabolized the larger cell's waste products and derived more energy. Part of this excess energy was returned to the host. The smaller cell replicated inside the larger one. Soon, a stable [symbiosis](/wiki/Symbiosis "Symbiosis") developed between the large cell and the smaller cells inside it. Over time, the host cell acquired some genes from the smaller cells, and the two kinds became dependent on each other: the larger cell could not survive without the energy produced by the smaller ones, and these, in turn, could not survive without the raw materials provided by the larger cell. The whole cell is now considered a single [organism](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), and the smaller cells are classified as [organelles](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle") called mitochondria.[[123]](#cite_note-BerkeleyEvolution-124)

A similar event occurred with [photosynthetic](/wiki/Photosynthetic "Photosynthetic") [cyanobacteria](/wiki/Cyanobacteria "Cyanobacteria")[[124]](#cite_note-Bergland-125) entering large [heterotrophic](/wiki/Heterotrophic "Heterotrophic") cells and becoming chloroplasts.[[112]](#cite_note-ForteyDtL-113): 60–61[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 536–539 Probably as a result of these changes, a line of cells capable of photosynthesis split off from the other eukaryotes more than 1 billion years ago. There were probably several such inclusion events. Besides the well-established [endosymbiotic theory](/wiki/Endosymbiotic_theory "Endosymbiotic theory") of the cellular origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts, there are theories that cells led to [peroxisomes](/wiki/Peroxisomes "Peroxisomes"), [spirochetes](/wiki/Spirochete "Spirochete") led to [cilia](/wiki/Cilia "Cilia") and [flagella](/wiki/Flagella "Flagella"), and that perhaps a [DNA virus](/wiki/DNA_virus "DNA virus") led to the cell nucleus,[[126]](#cite_note-takemura-127)[[127]](#cite_note-bell-128) though none of them are widely accepted.[[128]](#cite_note-peroxisome-129)

Archaeans, bacteria, and eukaryotes continued to diversify and to become more complex and better adapted to their environments. Each domain repeatedly split into multiple lineages. Around 1.1 Ga, the [plant](/wiki/Plant "Plant"), [animal](/wiki/Animal "Animal"), and [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi") lines had split, though they still existed as solitary cells. Some of these lived in colonies, and gradually a [division of labor](/wiki/Division_of_labor "Division of labor") began to take place; for instance, cells on the periphery might have started to assume different roles from those in the interior. Although the division between a colony with specialized cells and a multicellular organism is not always clear, around 1 billion years ago[[129]](#cite_note-Cosmic-evolution-bio2-130), the first multicellular plants emerged, probably [green algae](/wiki/Green_algae "Green algae").[[130]](#cite_note-bhattacharya-131) Possibly by around 900 Ma[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 488 true multicellularity had also evolved in animals.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

At first, it probably resembled today's [sponges](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge"), which have [totipotent](/wiki/Totipotent "Totipotent") cells that allow a disrupted organism to reassemble itself.[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 483–487 As the division of labor was completed in the different lineages of multicellular organisms, cells became more specialized and more dependent on each other.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

### Supercontinents in the Proterozoic

Main article: [Supercontinent cycle](/wiki/Supercontinent_cycle "Supercontinent cycle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Positions_of_ancient_continents%2C_550_million_years_ago.jpg/250px-Positions_of_ancient_continents%2C_550_million_years_ago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Positions_of_ancient_continents,_550_million_years_ago.jpg)

A reconstruction of Pannotia (550 Ma).

Reconstructions of tectonic plate movement in the past 250 million years (the Cenozoic and Mesozoic eras) can be made reliably using fitting of continental margins, ocean floor magnetic anomalies and [paleomagnetic poles](/wiki/Paleomagnetic_pole "Paleomagnetic pole"). No ocean crust dates back further than that, so earlier reconstructions are more difficult. Paleomagnetic poles are supplemented by geologic evidence such as [orogenic belts](/wiki/Orogenic_belt "Orogenic belt"), which mark the edges of ancient plates, and past distributions of flora and fauna. The further back in time, the scarcer and harder to interpret the data get and the more uncertain the reconstructions.[[133]](#cite_note-Kearey-134): 370

Throughout the history of Earth, there have been times when continents collided and formed a supercontinent, which later broke up into new continents. About 1000 to 830 Ma, most continental mass was united in the supercontinent Rodinia.[[133]](#cite_note-Kearey-134): 370[[134]](#cite_note-Torsvik2003-135) Rodinia may have been preceded by Early-Middle Proterozoic continents called Nuna and Columbia.[[133]](#cite_note-Kearey-134): 374[[135]](#cite_note-Zhao1-136)[[136]](#cite_note-Zhao2-137)

After the break-up of Rodinia about 800 Ma, the continents may have formed another short-lived supercontinent around 550 Ma. The hypothetical supercontinent is sometimes referred to as [Pannotia or Vendia](/wiki/Pannotia "Pannotia").[[137]](#cite_note-McElhinny-138): 321–322 The evidence for it is a phase of [continental collision](/wiki/Continental_collision "Continental collision") known as the [Pan-African orogeny](/wiki/Pan-African_orogeny "Pan-African orogeny"), which joined the continental masses of current-day Africa, South America, Antarctica and Australia. The existence of Pannotia depends on the timing of the rifting between [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") (which included most of the landmass now in the Southern Hemisphere, as well as the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") and the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent")) and [Laurentia](/wiki/Laurentia "Laurentia") (roughly equivalent to current-day North America).[[133]](#cite_note-Kearey-134): 374 It is at least certain that by the end of the Proterozoic eon, most of the continental mass lay united in a position around the south pole.[[138]](#cite_note-Dalziel-139)

### Late Proterozoic climate and life

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Spriggina_Floundensi_4.png/250px-Spriggina_Floundensi_4.png)](/wiki/File:Spriggina_Floundensi_4.png)

A 580 million year old fossil of *[Spriggina floundensi](/wiki/Spriggina "Spriggina")*, an animal from the [Ediacaran](/wiki/Ediacaran "Ediacaran") period. Such life forms could have been ancestors to the many new forms that originated in the [Cambrian Explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_Explosion "Cambrian Explosion").

The end of the Proterozoic saw at least two Snowball Earths, so severe that the surface of the oceans may have been completely frozen. This happened about 716.5 and 635 Ma, in the [Cryogenian period](/wiki/Cryogenian_period "Cryogenian period").[[139]](#cite_note-140) The intensity and mechanism of both glaciations are still under investigation and harder to explain than the early Proterozoic Snowball Earth.[[140]](#cite_note-Science_Daily_Snowball_Earth_challenged-141)
Most paleoclimatologists think the cold episodes were linked to the formation of the supercontinent Rodinia.[[141]](#cite_note-Hoffman-142) Because Rodinia was centered on the equator, rates of [chemical weathering](/wiki/Chemical_weathering "Chemical weathering") increased and carbon dioxide (CO2) was taken from the atmosphere. Because CO2 is an important greenhouse gas, climates cooled globally.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

In the same way, during the Snowball Earths most of the continental surface was covered with [permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost"), which decreased chemical weathering again, leading to the end of the glaciations. An alternative hypothesis is that enough carbon dioxide escaped through volcanic outgassing that the resulting greenhouse effect raised global temperatures.[[141]](#cite_note-Hoffman-142) Increased volcanic activity resulted from the break-up of Rodinia at about the same time.[[143]](#cite_note-144)

The Cryogenian period was followed by the [Ediacaran](/wiki/Ediacaran "Ediacaran") period, which was characterized by a rapid development of new multicellular lifeforms.[[144]](#cite_note-145) Whether there is a connection between the end of the severe ice ages and the increase in diversity of life is not clear, but it does not seem coincidental. The new forms of life, called Ediacara biota, were larger and more diverse than ever. Though the taxonomy of most Ediacaran life forms is unclear, some were ancestors of groups of modern life.[[145]](#cite_note-Xiao-146) Important developments were the origin of muscular and neural cells. None of the Ediacaran fossils had hard body parts like skeletons. These first appear after the boundary between the Proterozoic and [Phanerozoic](/wiki/Phanerozoic "Phanerozoic") eons or Ediacaran and Cambrian periods.[[146]](#cite_note-147)

## Phanerozoic Eon

Main article: [Phanerozoic](/wiki/Phanerozoic "Phanerozoic")

The Phanerozoic is the current eon on Earth, which started approximately 538.8 million years ago. It consists of three eras: The [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic"), [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic"), and [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic"),[[105]](#cite_note-StratChart_2022-106) and is the time when multi-cellular life greatly diversified into almost all the organisms known today.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

The Paleozoic ("old life") era was the first and longest era of the Phanerozoic eon, lasting from 538.8 to 251.9 Ma.[[105]](#cite_note-StratChart_2022-106) During the Paleozoic, many modern groups of life came into existence. Life colonized the land, first plants, then animals. Two significant extinctions occurred. The continents formed at the break-up of Pannotia and Rodinia at the end of the Proterozoic slowly moved together again, forming the supercontinent [Pangaea](/wiki/Pangaea "Pangaea") in the late Paleozoic.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

The Mesozoic ("middle life") era lasted from 251.9 Ma to 66 Ma.[[105]](#cite_note-StratChart_2022-106) It is subdivided into the [Triassic](/wiki/Triassic "Triassic"), [Jurassic](/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic"), and [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") periods. The era began with the [Permian–Triassic extinction event](/wiki/Permian%E2%80%93Triassic_extinction_event "Permian–Triassic extinction event"), the most severe extinction event in the fossil record whereby 95% of species on Earth died out,[[149]](#cite_note-bbc-permian-triassic-150) and ended with the [Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event](/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event "Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event") that wiped out the [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur").[[150]](#cite_note-151)

The Cenozoic ("new life") era began at 66 Ma, and is subdivided into the [Paleogene](/wiki/Paleogene "Paleogene"), [Neogene](/wiki/Neogene "Neogene"), and Quaternary periods. These three periods are further split into seven subdivisions, with the Paleogene composed of The [Paleocene](/wiki/Paleocene "Paleocene"), [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene"), and [Oligocene](/wiki/Oligocene "Oligocene"), the Neogene divided into the [Miocene](/wiki/Miocene "Miocene"), [Pliocene](/wiki/Pliocene "Pliocene"), and the Quaternary composed of the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene"), and Holocene.[[151]](#cite_note-152) Mammals, birds, amphibians, crocodilians, turtles, and lepidosaurs survived the Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event that killed off the non-avian dinosaurs and many other forms of life, and this is the era during which they diversified into their modern forms.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

### Tectonics, paleogeography and climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Pangaea_continents.svg/250px-Pangaea_continents.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pangaea_continents.svg)

[Pangaea](/wiki/Pangaea "Pangaea") was a [supercontinent](/wiki/Supercontinent "Supercontinent") that existed from about 300 to 180 Ma. The outlines of the modern continents and other landmasses are indicated on this map.

At the end of the Proterozoic, the supercontinent Pannotia had broken apart into the smaller continents Laurentia, [Baltica](/wiki/Baltica "Baltica"), [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia_(continent) "Siberia (continent)") and Gondwana.[[153]](#cite_note-154) During periods when continents move apart, more oceanic crust is formed by volcanic activity. Because the young volcanic crust is relatively hotter and less dense than the old oceanic crust, the ocean floors rise during such periods. This causes the [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") to rise. Therefore, in the first half of the Paleozoic, large areas of the continents were below sea level.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Early Paleozoic climates were warmer than today, but the end of the Ordovician saw a short [ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age") during which glaciers covered the south pole, where the huge continent Gondwana was situated. Traces of glaciation from this period are only found on former Gondwana. During the Late Ordovician ice age, a few mass extinctions took place, in which many [brachiopods](/wiki/Brachiopod "Brachiopod"), trilobites, [Bryozoa](/wiki/Bryozoa "Bryozoa") and [corals](/wiki/Coral "Coral") disappeared. These marine species could probably not contend with the decreasing temperature of the sea water.[[154]](#cite_note-bbc-ordovician-155)

The continents Laurentia and Baltica collided between 450 and 400 Ma, during the [Caledonian Orogeny](/wiki/Caledonian_Orogeny "Caledonian Orogeny"), to form [Laurussia](/wiki/Laurussia "Laurussia") (also known as Euramerica).[[155]](#cite_note-156) Traces of the mountain belt this collision caused can be found in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), and the northern [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia"). In the [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") period (416–359 Ma)[[21]](#cite_note-shortTimeScale-21) Gondwana and Siberia began to move towards Laurussia. The collision of Siberia with Laurussia caused the [Uralian Orogeny](/wiki/Uralian_orogeny "Uralian orogeny"), the collision of Gondwana with Laurussia is called the [Variscan or Hercynian Orogeny](/wiki/Variscan_Orogeny "Variscan Orogeny") in Europe or the [Alleghenian Orogeny](/wiki/Alleghenian_Orogeny "Alleghenian Orogeny") in North America. The latter phase took place during the [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") period (359–299 Ma)[[21]](#cite_note-shortTimeScale-21) and resulted in the formation of the last supercontinent, Pangaea.[[60]](#cite_note-Condie-61)

By 180 Ma, Pangaea broke up into [Laurasia](/wiki/Laurasia "Laurasia") and Gondwana.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Cambrian explosion

Main article: [Cambrian explosion](/wiki/Cambrian_explosion "Cambrian explosion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Elrathia_kingii_growth_series.jpg/250px-Elrathia_kingii_growth_series.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elrathia_kingii_growth_series.jpg)

[Trilobites](/wiki/Trilobites "Trilobites") first appeared during the Cambrian period and were among the most widespread and diverse groups of Paleozoic organisms.

The rate of the evolution of life as recorded by fossils accelerated in the [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian") period (542–488 Ma).[[21]](#cite_note-shortTimeScale-21) The sudden emergence of many new species, [phyla](/wiki/Phylum "Phylum"), and forms in this period is called the Cambrian Explosion. It was a form of [adaptive radiation](/wiki/Adaptive_radiation "Adaptive radiation"), where vacant [niches](/wiki/Ecological_niche "Ecological niche") left by the extinct [Ediacaran biota](/wiki/Ediacaran_biota "Ediacaran biota") were filled up by the emergence of new phyla.[[156]](#cite_note-Wille2008-157) The biological fomenting in the Cambrian Explosion was unprecedented before and since that time.[[59]](#cite_note-Lunine-60): 229 Whereas the Ediacaran life forms appear yet primitive and not easy to put in any modern group, at the end of the Cambrian, most modern phyla were already present. The development of hard body parts such as shells, [skeletons](/wiki/Skeleton "Skeleton") or [exoskeletons](/wiki/Exoskeleton "Exoskeleton") in animals like [molluscs](/wiki/Mollusc "Mollusc"), [echinoderms](/wiki/Echinoderm "Echinoderm"), [crinoids](/wiki/Crinoid "Crinoid") and [arthropods](/wiki/Arthropod "Arthropod") (a well-known group of arthropods from the lower Paleozoic are the [trilobites](/wiki/Trilobite "Trilobite")) made the preservation and [fossilization](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") of such life forms easier than those of their Proterozoic ancestors. For this reason, much more is known about life in and after the Cambrian period than about life in older periods. Some of these Cambrian groups appear complex but are seemingly quite different from modern life; examples are *[Anomalocaris](/wiki/Anomalocaris "Anomalocaris")* and *[Haikouichthys](/wiki/Haikouichthys "Haikouichthys")*. More recently, however, these seem to have found a place in modern classification.[[157]](#cite_note-158)

During the Cambrian, the first [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") animals, among them the first [fishes](/wiki/Fishes "Fishes"), had appeared.[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 357 A creature that could have been the ancestor of the fishes, or was probably closely related to it, was *[Pikaia](/wiki/Pikaia "Pikaia")*. It had a primitive [notochord](/wiki/Notochord "Notochord"), a structure that could have developed into a [vertebral column](/wiki/Vertebral_column "Vertebral column") later. The first fishes with [jaws](/wiki/Jaw "Jaw") ([Gnathostomata](/wiki/Gnathostomata "Gnathostomata")) appeared during the next geological period, the [Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician"). The colonisation of new [niches](/wiki/Ecological_niche "Ecological niche") resulted in massive body sizes. In this way, fishes with increasing sizes evolved during the early Paleozoic, such as the titanic [placoderm](/wiki/Placoderm "Placoderm") *[Dunkleosteus](/wiki/Dunkleosteus "Dunkleosteus")*, which could grow 7 meters (23 ft) long.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

The diversity of life forms did not increase significantly because of a series of mass extinctions that define widespread biostratigraphic units called *[biomeres](/w/index.php?title=Biomeres&action=edit&redlink=1 "Biomeres (page does not exist)")*.[[159]](#cite_note-Runkel-160) After each extinction pulse, the [continental shelf](/wiki/Continental_shelf "Continental shelf") regions were repopulated by similar life forms that may have been evolving slowly elsewhere.[[160]](#cite_note-Palmer-161) By the late Cambrian, the trilobites had reached their greatest diversity and dominated nearly all fossil assemblages.[[161]](#cite_note-Hallam-162): 34

### Colonization of land

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Devonianscene-green.jpg/250px-Devonianscene-green.jpg)](/wiki/File:Devonianscene-green.jpg)

Artist's conception of [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") flora

Oxygen accumulation from photosynthesis resulted in the formation of an ozone layer that absorbed much of the Sun's [ultraviolet radiation](/wiki/Ultraviolet_radiation "Ultraviolet radiation"), meaning unicellular organisms that reached land were less likely to die, and prokaryotes began to multiply and become better adapted to survival out of the water. Prokaryote lineages had probably colonized the land as early as 3 Ga[[162]](#cite_note-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164) even before the origin of the eukaryotes. For a long time, the land remained barren of multicellular organisms. The supercontinent Pannotia formed around 600 Ma and then broke apart a short 50 million years later.[[164]](#cite_note-liebermean-165) Fish, the [earliest vertebrates](/wiki/Prehistoric_fish "Prehistoric fish"), evolved in the oceans around 530 Ma.[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 354 A major [extinction event](/wiki/Cambrian%E2%80%93Ordovician_extinction_events "Cambrian–Ordovician extinction events") occurred near the end of the Cambrian period,[[165]](#cite_note-bbc-cambrian-166) which ended 488 Ma.[[166]](#cite_note-landing-167)

Several hundred million years ago, plants (probably resembling [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae")) and fungi started growing at the edges of the water and then out of it.[[167]](#cite_note-Fortey-168): 138–140 The oldest fossils of land fungi and plants date to 480–460 Ma, though molecular evidence suggests the fungi may have colonized the land as early as 1000 Ma and the plants 700 Ma.[[168]](#cite_note-heckman-169) Initially remaining close to the water's edge, mutations and variations resulted in further colonization of this new environment. The timing of the first animals to leave the oceans is not precisely known: the oldest clear evidence is of arthropods on land around 450 Ma,[[169]](#cite_note-johnson-170) perhaps thriving and becoming better adapted due to the vast food source provided by the terrestrial plants. There is also unconfirmed evidence that arthropods may have appeared on land as early as 530 Ma.[[170]](#cite_note-macnaughton-171)

### Evolution of tetrapods

Further information: [Evolution of tetrapods](/wiki/Evolution_of_tetrapods "Evolution of tetrapods")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Tiktaalik_BW.jpg/250px-Tiktaalik_BW.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiktaalik_BW.jpg)

*[Tiktaalik](/wiki/Tiktaalik "Tiktaalik")*, a fish with limb-like fins and a predecessor of tetrapods. Reconstruction from fossils about 375 million years old.

At the end of the Ordovician period, 443 Ma,[[21]](#cite_note-shortTimeScale-21) additional [extinction events](/wiki/Ordovician%E2%80%93Silurian_extinction_event "Ordovician–Silurian extinction event") occurred, perhaps due to a concurrent ice age.[[154]](#cite_note-bbc-ordovician-155) Around 380 to 375 Ma, the first [tetrapods](/wiki/Tetrapod "Tetrapod") evolved from fish.[[171]](#cite_note-clack-sa-172) Fins evolved to become limbs that the first tetrapods used to lift their heads out of the water to breathe air. This would let them live in oxygen-poor water, or pursue small prey in shallow water.[[171]](#cite_note-clack-sa-172) They may have later ventured on land for brief periods. Eventually, some of them became so well adapted to terrestrial life that they spent their adult lives on land, although they hatched in the water and returned to lay their eggs. This was the origin of the [amphibians](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian"). About 365 Ma, another [period of extinction](/wiki/Late_Devonian_extinction "Late Devonian extinction") occurred, perhaps as a result of [global cooling](/wiki/Global_cooling "Global cooling").[[172]](#cite_note-173) Plants evolved [seeds](/wiki/Seed "Seed"), which dramatically accelerated their spread on land, around this time (by approximately 360 Ma).[[173]](#cite_note-willis-174)[[174]](#cite_note-waikato-175)

About 20 million years later (340 Ma[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 293–296), the [amniotic egg](/wiki/Amniotic_egg "Amniotic egg") evolved, which could be laid on land, giving a survival advantage to tetrapod embryos. This resulted in the divergence of [amniotes](/wiki/Amniote "Amniote") from amphibians. Another 30 million years (310 Ma[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 254–256) saw the divergence of the [synapsids](/wiki/Synapsid "Synapsid") (including mammals) from the [sauropsids](/wiki/Sauropsid "Sauropsid") (including birds and reptiles). Other groups of organisms continued to evolve, and lines diverged—in fish, insects, bacteria, and so on—but less is known of the details.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Europasaurus_holgeri_Scene_2.jpg/250px-Europasaurus_holgeri_Scene_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europasaurus_holgeri_Scene_2.jpg)

[Dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") were the dominant terrestrial vertebrates throughout most of the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic")

After yet another, the most severe extinction of the period (251~250 Ma), around 230 Ma, dinosaurs split off from their reptilian ancestors.[[175]](#cite_note-bbc-new_blood-176) The [Triassic–Jurassic extinction event](/wiki/Triassic%E2%80%93Jurassic_extinction_event "Triassic–Jurassic extinction event") at 200 Ma spared many of the dinosaurs,[[21]](#cite_note-shortTimeScale-21)[[176]](#cite_note-bbc-triassic-177) and they soon became dominant among the vertebrates. Though some mammalian lines began to separate during this period, existing mammals were probably small animals resembling [shrews](/wiki/Shrew "Shrew").[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 169

The boundary between avian and non-avian dinosaurs is unclear, but *[Archaeopteryx](/wiki/Archaeopteryx "Archaeopteryx")*, traditionally considered one of the first birds, lived around 150 Ma.[[177]](#cite_note-archaeopteryx-178)

The earliest evidence for the [angiosperms](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") evolving flowers is during the Cretaceous period, some 20 million years later (132 Ma).[[178]](#cite_note-tol-angiosperms-179)

### Extinctions

The first of five great mass extinctions was the [Ordovician-Silurian extinction](/wiki/Ordovician-Silurian_extinction "Ordovician-Silurian extinction"). Its possible cause was the intense glaciation of Gondwana, which eventually led to a [Snowball Earth](/wiki/Snowball_Earth "Snowball Earth"). 60% of marine invertebrates became extinct, and 25% of all families.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The second mass extinction was the [Late Devonian extinction](/wiki/Late_Devonian_extinction "Late Devonian extinction"), probably caused by the evolution of trees, which could have led to the depletion of greenhouse gases (like CO2) or the [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication") of water. 70% of all species became extinct.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

The third mass extinction was the Permian-Triassic, or the [Great Dying](/wiki/Great_Dying "Great Dying"), event. The event was possibly caused by some combination of the [Siberian Traps](/wiki/Siberian_Traps "Siberian Traps") volcanic event, an asteroid impact, [methane hydrate](/wiki/Methane_clathrate "Methane clathrate") gasification, sea level fluctuations, and a major [anoxic event](/wiki/Anoxic_event "Anoxic event"). Either the proposed [Wilkes Land crater](/wiki/Wilkes_Land_crater "Wilkes Land crater")[[180]](#cite_note-bbc-antarctic-crater-181) in Antarctica or [Bedout structure](/wiki/Bedout "Bedout") off the northwest coast of Australia may indicate an impact connection with the Permian-Triassic extinction. But it remains uncertain whether these or other proposed Permian-Triassic boundary craters are real impact craters or even contemporary with the Permian-Triassic extinction event. This was by far the deadliest extinction ever, with about 57% of all [families](/wiki/Family_(biology) "Family (biology)") and 83% of all [genera](/wiki/Genus "Genus") killed.[[181]](#cite_note-Benton-182)[[182]](#cite_note-BergstromDugatkin2012-183)

The fourth mass extinction was the [Triassic-Jurassic extinction event](/wiki/Triassic-Jurassic_extinction_event "Triassic-Jurassic extinction event") in which almost all [synapsids](/wiki/Synapsids "Synapsids") and [archosaurs](/wiki/Archosaurs "Archosaurs") became extinct, probably due to new competition from dinosaurs.[[183]](#cite_note-184)

The fifth and most recent mass extinction was the [Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event](/wiki/Cretaceous-Paleogene_extinction_event "Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event"). In 66 Ma, a 10-kilometer (6.2 mi) [asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") struck Earth just off the [Yucatán Peninsula](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n_Peninsula "Yucatán Peninsula")—somewhere in the southwestern tip of then Laurasia—where the [Chicxulub crater](/wiki/Chicxulub_crater "Chicxulub crater") is today. This ejected vast quantities of particulate matter and vapor into the air that occluded sunlight, inhibiting photosynthesis. 75% of all life, including the non-avian dinosaurs, became extinct,[[184]](#cite_note-cosmic-evolution-bio4-185) marking the end of the Cretaceous period and Mesozoic era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Diversification of mammals

Further information: [Evolution of mammals](/wiki/Evolution_of_mammals "Evolution of mammals")

The first true mammals evolved in the shadows of dinosaurs and other large archosaurs that filled the world by the late Triassic. The first mammals were very small, and were probably nocturnal to escape predation. Mammal diversification truly began only after the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event.[[185]](#cite_note-186) By the early Paleocene Earth recovered from the extinction, and mammalian diversity increased. Creatures like *[Ambulocetus](/wiki/Ambulocetus "Ambulocetus")* took to the oceans to eventually evolve into whales,[[186]](#cite_note-187) whereas some creatures, like primates, took to the trees.[[187]](#cite_note-188) This all changed during the mid to late Eocene when the circum-Antarctic current formed between Antarctica and Australia which disrupted weather patterns on a global scale. Grassless [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") began to predominate much of the landscape, and mammals such as *[Andrewsarchus](/wiki/Andrewsarchus "Andrewsarchus")* rose up to become the largest known terrestrial predatory mammal ever,[[188]](#cite_note-189) and [early whales](/wiki/Archaeoceti "Archaeoceti") like *[Basilosaurus](/wiki/Basilosaurus "Basilosaurus")* took control of the seas. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The evolution of [grasses](/wiki/Poaceae "Poaceae") brought a remarkable change to Earth's landscape, and the new open spaces pushed mammals to get bigger and bigger. Grass started to expand in the Miocene, and the Miocene is where many modern- day mammals first appeared. Giant [ungulates](/wiki/Ungulates "Ungulates") like *[Paraceratherium](/wiki/Paraceratherium "Paraceratherium")* and *[Deinotherium](/wiki/Deinotherium "Deinotherium")* evolved to rule the grasslands. The evolution of grass also brought [primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate") down from the trees, and started [human evolution](/wiki/Human_evolution "Human evolution"). The first big cats evolved during this time as well.[[189]](#cite_note-190) The [Tethys Sea](/wiki/Tethys_Sea "Tethys Sea") was closed off by the collision of Africa and Europe.[[190]](#cite_note-191)

The formation of Panama was perhaps the most important geological event to occur in the last 60 million years. Atlantic and Pacific currents were closed off from each other, which caused the formation of the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream"), which made Europe warmer. The land bridge allowed the isolated creatures of South America to migrate over to North America and vice versa.[[191]](#cite_note-192) Various species migrated south, leading to the presence in South America of [llamas](/wiki/Llama "Llama"), the [spectacled bear](/wiki/Spectacled_bear "Spectacled bear"), [kinkajous](/wiki/Kinkajou "Kinkajou") and [jaguars](/wiki/Jaguar "Jaguar").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Three million years ago saw the start of the Pleistocene epoch, which featured dramatic climatic changes due to the ice ages. The ice ages led to the evolution and expansion of modern man in Saharan Africa. The mega-fauna that dominated fed on grasslands that, by now, had taken over much of the subtropical world. The large amounts of water held in the ice allowed various water bodies to shrink and sometimes disappear, such as the North Sea and the Bering Strait. It is believed by many that a huge migration took place along [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia"), which is why, today, there are [camels](/wiki/Camels "Camels") (which evolved and became extinct in North America), [horses](/wiki/Horses "Horses") (which evolved and became extinct in North America), and Native Americans. The end of the last ice age coincided with the expansion of man and a massive die out of ice age mega-fauna.

Main article: [Human evolution](/wiki/Human_evolution "Human evolution")

A small African ape living around 6 Ma was the last animal whose descendants would include both modern humans and their closest relatives, the [chimpanzees](/wiki/Pan_(genus) "Pan (genus)").[[101]](#cite_note-:0-102)[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 100–101 Only two branches of its family tree have surviving descendants. Very soon after the split, for reasons that are still unclear, apes in one branch developed the ability to [walk upright](/wiki/Bipedal "Bipedal").[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 95–99 [Brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") size increased rapidly, and by 2 Ma, the first animals classified in the genus *[Homo](/wiki/Homo "Homo")* had appeared.[[167]](#cite_note-Fortey-168): 300 Around the same time, the other branch split into the ancestors of the [common chimpanzee](/wiki/Common_chimpanzee "Common chimpanzee") and the ancestors of the [bonobo](/wiki/Bonobo "Bonobo") as evolution continued simultaneously in all life forms.[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 100–101

The ability to control [fire](/wiki/Fire "Fire") probably began in *[Homo erectus](/wiki/Homo_erectus "Homo erectus")* (or *[Homo ergaster](/wiki/Homo_ergaster "Homo ergaster")*), probably at least 790,000 years ago[[192]](#cite_note-goren-inbar-193) but perhaps as early as 1.5 Ma.[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 67 The [use and discovery of controlled fire](/wiki/Control_of_fire_by_early_humans "Control of fire by early humans") may even predate *Homo erectus*. Fire was possibly used by the early [Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic") ([Oldowan](/wiki/Oldowan "Oldowan")) hominid *[Homo habilis](/wiki/Homo_habilis "Homo habilis")* or strong [australopithecines](/wiki/Australopithecine "Australopithecine") such as *[Paranthropus](/wiki/Paranthropus "Paranthropus").*[[193]](#cite_note-McClellan-194)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Human_evolution_chart-en.svg/250px-Human_evolution_chart-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Human_evolution_chart-en.svg)

A reconstruction of human history based on fossil data.[[194]](#cite_note-195)

It is more difficult to establish the [origin of language](/wiki/Origin_of_language "Origin of language"); it is unclear whether *Homo erectus* could speak or if that capability had not begun until *Homo sapiens*.[[125]](#cite_note-Dawkins-Ancestors-126): 67 As brain size increased, babies were born earlier, before their heads grew too large to pass through the [pelvis](/wiki/Pelvis "Pelvis"). As a result, they exhibited more [plasticity](/wiki/Neuroplasticity "Neuroplasticity"), thus possessing an increased capacity to learn and requiring a longer period of dependence. Social skills became more complex, language became more sophisticated, and tools became more elaborate. This contributed to further cooperation and intellectual development.[[195]](#cite_note-McNeill-196): 7 Modern humans (*[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")*) are believed to have originated around 200,000 years ago or earlier [in Africa](/wiki/Single-origin_hypothesis "Single-origin hypothesis"); the oldest fossils date back to around 160,000 years ago.[[196]](#cite_note-gibbons-197)

The first humans to show signs of [spirituality](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality") are the [Neanderthals](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") (usually classified as a separate species with no surviving descendants); they buried their dead, often with no sign of food or tools.[[197]](#cite_note-hopfe-198): 17 However, evidence of more sophisticated beliefs, such as the early [Cro-Magnon](/wiki/Cro-Magnon "Cro-Magnon") [cave paintings](/wiki/Cave_painting "Cave painting") (probably with magical or religious significance)[[197]](#cite_note-hopfe-198): 17–19 did not appear until 32,000 years ago.[[198]](#cite_note-Chauvet-199) Cro-Magnons also left behind stone figurines such as [Venus of Willendorf](/wiki/Venus_of_Willendorf "Venus of Willendorf"), probably also signifying religious belief.[[197]](#cite_note-hopfe-198): 17–19 By 11,000 years ago, *Homo sapiens* had reached the southern tip of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), the last of the uninhabited continents (except for Antarctica, which remained undiscovered [until 1820 AD](/wiki/Antarctica#19th_century "Antarctica")).[[199]](#cite_note-oxford-atlas-200) Tool use and communication continued to improve, and interpersonal relationships became more intricate.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*[Vitruvian Man](/wiki/Vitruvian_Man "Vitruvian Man")*, c. 1490 by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), epitomizes the advances in art and science seen during the Renaissance.

Throughout more than 90% of its history, *Homo sapiens* lived in small bands as [nomadic](/wiki/Nomad "Nomad") [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer").[[195]](#cite_note-McNeill-196): 8 As language became more complex, the ability to remember and communicate information resulted in a new replicator: the [meme](/wiki/Meme "Meme").[[200]](#cite_note-dawkins-sg-201) Ideas could be exchanged quickly and passed down the generations. [Cultural evolution](/wiki/Cultural_evolution "Cultural evolution") quickly outpaced [biological evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution"), and [history](/wiki/History "History") proper began. Between 8500 and 7000 [BC](/wiki/Before_Christ "Before Christ"), humans in the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent") in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") began the systematic husbandry of plants and animals: [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture").[[201]](#cite_note-Tudge-202) This spread to neighboring regions and developed independently elsewhere until most *Homo sapiens* lived sedentary lives in permanent settlements as farmers. Not all societies abandoned nomadism, especially those in isolated areas of the globe poor in [domesticable](/wiki/Domestication "Domestication") plant species, such as [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia").[[202]](#cite_note-diamond-203) However, among those civilizations that did adopt agriculture, the relative stability and increased productivity provided by farming allowed the population to expand.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Agriculture had a major impact; humans began to affect the environment as never before. Surplus food allowed a priestly or governing class to arise, followed by increasing [division of labor](/wiki/Division_of_labour "Division of labour"). This led to Earth's [first civilization](/wiki/First_civilization "First civilization") at [Sumer](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") in the Middle East, between 4000 and 3000 BC.[[195]](#cite_note-McNeill-196): 15 Additional civilizations quickly arose in [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), at the [Indus River valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") and in China. The invention of [writing](/wiki/Writing "Writing") enabled complex societies to arise: record-keeping and [libraries](/wiki/Library "Library") served as a storehouse of knowledge and increased the cultural transmission of information. Humans no longer had to spend all their time working for survival, enabling the first specialized occupations (e.g. craftsmen, merchants, priests, etc.). Curiosity and education drove the pursuit of knowledge and wisdom, and various disciplines, including [science](/wiki/Science "Science") (in a primitive form), arose. This in turn led to the emergence of increasingly larger and more complex civilizations, such as the first empires, which at times traded with one another, or fought for territory and resources.

By around 500 BC, there were advanced civilizations in the Middle East, Iran, India, China, and Greece, at times expanding, at times entering into decline.[[195]](#cite_note-McNeill-196): 3 In 221 BC, China became a single polity that would grow to spread its culture throughout [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and it has remained the most populous nation in the world. During this period, famous [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") texts known as [vedas](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") came in existence in [Indus Valley Civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilization "Indus Valley Civilization"). This civilization developed in [warfare](/wiki/Warfare "Warfare"), [arts](/wiki/Arts "Arts"), [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), mathematics and [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The fundamentals of [Western civilization](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") were largely shaped in [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), with the world's first [democratic government](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") and major advances in philosophy and [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), and in [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") with advances in law, government, and engineering.[[203]](#cite_note-Daly2013-204)

The [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") was [Christianized by Emperor Constantine](/wiki/Religion_in_ancient_Rome#History_of_Roman_religion "Religion in ancient Rome") in the early 4th century and [declined](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Roman_Empire "Decline of the Roman Empire") by the end of the 5th. Beginning with the 7th century, [Christianization of Europe](/wiki/Christianization#Christianization_of_Europe_(6th–9th_centuries) "Christianization") began, and since at least the 4th century [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") has played a [prominent role](/wiki/Role_of_Christianity_in_civilization "Role of Christianity in civilization") in the [shaping of Western civilization](/wiki/History_of_Western_civilization "History of Western civilization").[[204]](#cite_note-Caltron_J.H_Hayas-205)[[205]](#cite_note-How_The_Catholic_Church_Built_Western_Civilization-206)[[206]](#cite_note-Perry2012-207)[[207]](#cite_note-208) In 610, [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") was founded and quickly became the dominant religion in [Western Asia](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia"). The [House of Wisdom](/wiki/House_of_Wisdom "House of Wisdom") was established in [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid")-era [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[208]](#cite_note-209) It is considered to have been a major intellectual center during the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age"), where Muslim scholars in [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") and [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") flourished from the ninth to the thirteenth centuries until the [Mongol sack of Baghdad](/wiki/Mongol_sack_of_Baghdad "Mongol sack of Baghdad") in 1258 AD. In 1054 AD the [Great Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") between the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") led to the prominent cultural differences between [Western](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe").[[209]](#cite_note-BideleuxJeffries48-210)

In the 14th century, the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") began in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") with advances in religion, art, and science.[[195]](#cite_note-McNeill-196): 317–319 At that time the Christian Church as a political entity lost much of its power. In 1492, [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") reached the Americas, initiating great changes to the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"). European civilization began to change beginning in 1500, leading to the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") and [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"). That continent began to exert political and cultural [dominance](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") over human societies around the world, a time known as the [Colonial era](/wiki/History_of_colonialism "History of colonialism") (see also: [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery")).[[195]](#cite_note-McNeill-196): 295–299 In the 18th century a cultural movement known as the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") further shaped the mentality of Europe and contributed to its [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1776, the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States") declared independence from the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") in part because of the ideas popularized by The Enlightenment.[[210]](#cite_note-211) The resulting war contributed to a chain of events that led to the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars").[[211]](#cite_note-212) In the 20th century, [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") and [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") spread around the world setting the stage for [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [the Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), [the Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race"), [nuclear proliferation](/wiki/Nuclear_proliferation "Nuclear proliferation"), and [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization").[[212]](#cite_note-213) The 20th century also saw the rise of rapid technological advancements with humanity moving from the [Wright brothers](/wiki/Wright_brothers "Wright brothers")' first powered airplane flight in 1903, to the [Apollo 11 Moon landing](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") in 1969, to the [development of the internet](/wiki/History_of_the_Internet "History of the Internet") in the last few decades of the millenium. The human population also grew from 1 billion people in 1800 to over 8 billion today.[[213]](#cite_note-214)

In the 21st century, the planet continues to face the impact of human activities and technology. Today some scientists argue that because of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and other environmental impacts the Earth is now in the midst of a [6th mass extinction](/wiki/Holocene_extinction "Holocene extinction") and/or a new geological epoch called the [Anthropocene](/wiki/Anthropocene "Anthropocene") though these concepts are both disputed.[[214]](#cite_note-215)[[215]](#cite_note-216)[[216]](#cite_note-217)
